mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Australia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Australia IS its own continent. It is the only country in the world that takes up an entire continent 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Australia?
mapQuestions.answer: Canberra. Most people guess Sydney, but the real capital is Canberra, a planned city built halfway between Sydney and Melbourne because the two big cities could not agree which should be capital.

mapQuestions.question: What three oceans touch Australia?
mapQuestions.answer: The Indian Ocean on the west, the Pacific Ocean on the east, and the Southern Ocean to the south 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What is the famous huge red rock in the middle of Australia?
mapQuestions.answer: Uluru (also called Ayers Rock). It is sacred to the Anangu Aboriginal people, and as a sign of respect you are no longer allowed to climb it.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in mainland Australia?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Kosciuszko, only 2,228 metres. Australia is one of the flattest countries on Earth.

foods.name: Vegemite on toast
foods.description: Salty black yeast paste spread thinly on buttered toast. Most non-Aussies hate it on the first try. Aussies grew up on it and love it.

foods.name: Meat pie
foods.description: A handheld pie of minced beef in gravy, with a squiggle of tomato sauce on top. The unofficial national dish, especially at the footy.

foods.name: Lamington
foods.description: A square of sponge cake dipped in chocolate icing and rolled in desiccated coconut. Named after a colonial governor.

foods.name: Tim Tam
foods.description: Two chocolate biscuits with cream between, coated in chocolate. The proper way: bite both ends off, suck hot tea through it, then eat fast before it collapses.

foods.name: Pavlova
foods.description: A giant crispy-outside, soft-inside meringue topped with whipped cream and fresh fruit. New Zealand also claims it. Both countries argue about who invented it.

foods.name: Sausage sizzle
foods.description: A sausage in a slice of white bread with onions and tomato sauce. Sold to raise money outside hardware stores on Saturdays. Iconic.

scavengerItems.label: A real kangaroo (in the wild, in a park, or at least on a road sign)
A koala (asleep in a eucalyptus tree, usually)
A boomerang, in a shop, museum or gallery
A bright pink-and-grey galah, or a noisy white cockatoo, in a tree
A "beware of swooping magpies" or crocodile warning sign
A surfer carrying a board on a beach
A sausage sizzle outside a Bunnings or supermarket
Someone wearing a wide-brimmed Akubra (the real Aussie hat)
An Esky (cool box) at a barbecue or in the back of a ute
A road sign showing hundreds of km to the next town
A piece of Aboriginal dot painting on a wall, shop or canvas
A goanna, blue-tongued lizard, or any reptile sunning itself

geoMatches.description: The biggest city. Famous for the Opera House sails, the Harbour Bridge, and Bondi Beach. Built around one of the world's great natural harbours.
A massive red sandstone rock rising out of the desert in the centre of the country. Sacred to the Anangu Aboriginal people.
The largest living structure on Earth, stretching 2,300km along the north-east coast. Visible from space. Home to 1,500 fish species.
The vast red interior. Almost empty of people, full of strange rocks, red dust, kangaroos, and roads that run for hundreds of km between towns.
The big island state south of the mainland. Cool, green, mountainous, with some of the cleanest air in the world.
The southern city. Hidden laneways covered in street art, arguably the best coffee culture in the world, and four seasons in a single day.

senses.smell: Eucalyptus on a hot afternoon, sausages sizzling at a BBQ, sea salt, sunblock, freshly cut grass at a cricket ground, dust after summer rain…
senses.hear: A magpie warbling, a kookaburra laughing like a maniac, "G'day mate" everywhere, the slap of surf, cicadas in the heat, footy commentary on the radio…
senses.taste: Salty Vegemite on warm buttered toast, a snag in bread with onions, sweet pavlova with passionfruit, a melting Tim Tam, hot meat pie burning your tongue…
senses.feel: Hot sand under bare feet, the slap of ocean on your back, sticky sunblock, a southerly breeze in the evening, eucalyptus leaves crackling underfoot…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are there more kangaroos than people in Australia?
stories.body: There are around 26 million people in Australia, and around 50 million kangaroos. Kangaroos love the dry grasslands across the middle and south of the country, and they have very few natural predators left. They are the only big animal that travels by hopping. A big kangaroo can leap 9 metres in one bound and reach 60 km/h. Weirdly, they cannot walk backwards: their giant feet and thick tail make it impossible. That is part of why a kangaroo and an emu (which also cannot walk backwards) are on the Australian coat of arms, the country that only moves forward.

stories.question: What is the biggest living thing on Earth?
stories.body: It is the Great Barrier Reef, just off the north-east coast. It is not one reef but 2,900 separate ones, stretching for 2,300 km, about the same as London to Athens. It is so big it is the only living structure on Earth visible from space. The reef is home to 1,500 species of fish, 6 of the world's 7 species of sea turtle, dugongs, sharks, and 30 species of whales and dolphins. It is also in trouble: warmer seas are causing coral bleaching, where colourful coral turns ghost white and can die. Many scientists worry whether the reef will still be there for your grandchildren.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could protect just ONE part of nature from changing, anywhere in the world, what would you pick?

stories.question: Why do Australian magpies attack people?
stories.body: For most of the year the Australian magpie is a beautiful black-and-white bird with one of the most musical songs of any bird in the world. But during nesting season (about September to November), some male magpies SWOOP humans walking near their tree, to protect their chicks. They can recognise human faces and remember individuals for years, if a magpie decides you are a threat, every magpie in that family might too, season after season. Aussie kids handle it by drawing big eyes on the back of their bike helmets (magpies attack from behind, the fake eyes scare them off). Posties wear special spiked hats. The trick: walk slowly, do not run, and do not stare.

stories.question: How old are Aboriginal Australian cultures?
stories.body: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have lived continuously in Australia for at least 65,000 years, the oldest still-living cultures anywhere on Earth. Before Europeans arrived in 1788, there were hundreds of different language groups, each with their own land, songs, ceremonies and stories. The "Dreaming" or "Dreamtime" is a way of describing how the land, animals and laws were created by ancestor beings. Aboriginal art often uses dots and symbols to record these stories and to "map" country. Uluru, the giant red rock in the centre of Australia, is sacred to the Anangu people, who have lived around it for tens of thousands of years. Climbing it was officially banned in 2019 out of respect.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A culture that has lasted 65,000 years has lived through ice ages, sea-level changes, and many huge events. What do you think makes a culture last that long?

stories.question: Why is Australian wildlife so strange?
stories.body: About 100 million years ago, Australia broke away from a giant supercontinent called Gondwana and drifted slowly north on its own. Cut off from the rest of the world for tens of millions of years, mammals here evolved differently from the rest of the planet. That is why most native Australian mammals are marsupials, carrying tiny babies in a pouch (kangaroos, koalas, wombats, possums, Tasmanian devils). The platypus is even weirder: one of only 5 mammals on Earth that lay eggs, with a duck-like bill, otter feet, beaver tail, and venomous spurs on the back legs of the males. When scientists first sent one to London they thought it was a hoax stitched together by a clever taxidermist. Australia is also home to 21 of the 25 most venomous snakes in the world, plus deadly spiders, jellyfish, and a tiny blue-ringed octopus that can kill you in minutes.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten in Australia so far?
Have you seen a wild kangaroo or koala yet? What did you think?
What's one thing Aussies do really differently from where we live?
How hot does it actually feel here compared to back home?
If you had to live in just one spot — city, beach, or outback — which would it be?
Aboriginal cultures are 65,000+ years old. What do you think people 65,000 years from now will look back on as old?

wordSearch.hint: Australian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Eastern grey kangaroo
animals.description: Hopping across pastures and along golf courses at dawn and dusk. Everywhere outside the cities.

animals.name: Koala
animals.description: Asleep in a eucalyptus tree, usually. Try the Otways, Magnetic Island, Kangaroo Island.

animals.name: Rainbow lorikeet
animals.description: Loud, brightly-coloured parrots in every Aussie backyard and tree.

animals.name: Magpie or kookaburra
animals.description: Magpies for the songs (and swooping), kookaburras for the laughing call.

animals.name: Saltwater crocodile
animals.description: Northern Territory and far North Queensland. NEVER swim where you see "croc" warning signs.

animals.name: Platypus
animals.description: The egg-laying mammal. Best in Tasmanian streams or Eungella National Park in Queensland.
